text: The video shows a total laparoscopic hysterectomy being performed on a patient with a large uterus containing a 24-centimeter intramural fibroid on the fundus and multiple smaller fibroids. The uterus is visibly enlarged, extending up to the epigastric region, with the margin of the uterus clearly defined.

The surgeon demonstrates the entry technique for a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a large uterus, approximately ten centimeters above the umbilicus. The abdominal wall is stretched down, and a small stab wound is made at Palmer's point, two centimeters above the costal margin. A Veress needle is introduced perpendicular to the abdominal wall, followed by the telescope. The irrigation and suction tests are performed, and the hanging drop test is positive, indicating proper placement. Insufflation begins with a pressure of fifteen mmHg, gradually increasing the actual pressure to nine mmHg, with a flow rate initially at one liter per minute, then increasing to three liters per minute.

The surgeon observes homogeneous distension of the abdominal wall as pneumoperitoneum is completed. An incision is enlarged to 11 mm using an 11 number knife, and an 11 mm trocar is introduced at Palmer's point with a screwing motion. Mosquito forceps are used to dilate the wound. The telescope is then introduced for initial diagnostics, followed by the placement of an epigastric port just below the sternum.

The surgeon places an 11 mm epigastric port just below the GPH sternum, slightly left of the midline to avoid the falciform ligament. The telescope is transferred to this port, providing a view of the large uterus with visible fibroids. The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position, with the head and table lowered to facilitate access.

The surgeon identifies multiple large fibroids within the uterus, which is visibly heavy and drooping. The left ovary, fallopian tube, and round ligament are visible. A 5mm myoma screw is introduced through a contralateral port to manipulate the uterus, as the uterine manipulator is ineffective due to the size and weight of the fibroids.

The surgeon uses a contralateral port for diagnostic purposes due to the ineffectiveness of the uterine manipulator. The epigastric region houses the telescope, with additional ports in the right and left lumbar regions. The video shows multiple fibroids occupying the uterus, which fills the entire abdomen and pelvis. The right fallopian tube is visible, appearing varicose, alongside the numerous fibroids.

The surgeon inserts a myoma screw to pull the uterus towards the upper abdomen near the GP sternum area. Using a grasper and ligature, the surgeon begins dissecting the pedicles, starting with the round ligament, followed by the fallopian tube, and then the ovarian ligament.
A grasper is used on the left side to hold the tissue. The round ligament is visibly cut. The fallopian tube is then coagulated, and the procedure progresses to the ovarian ligament.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the ovarian ligament and proceeds to take a 2.5 cm section over the broad ligament on the right side. The procedure then shifts to the left side using the contralateral port, where the fallopian tube is dissected first.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw to pull the uterus towards the epigastric region, with the patient's head positioned downwards to aid in the maneuver. The left ovary and round ligament are visible, and fluid from a hydro salpinx is released. The ovarian ligament is dissected alongside the major ovarian and salpingeal structures.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the peritoneum of the uterovesical fold, while the left hand grasper stretches the tissue. The remaining part of the round ligament and a section of the broad ligament are manipulated from the contralateral port. The myoma screw pulls the uterus upward, facilitating the separation process.

The surgeon separates the bladder from surrounding tissues, including the bladder pillars, using lateral traction with the left hand to stretch the peritoneum. The procedure is focused on the right side.

The surgeon introduces a fourth port to facilitate the removal of the uterus from the lumbar area. On the left side, a bipolar instrument is used, while on the right side, a ligature is applied. This setup allows for effective control of any accidental bleeding by using two instruments simultaneously. The strategy involves first coagulating the tissue and then cutting it.
The surgeon coagulates the remaining part of the broad ligament before cutting it. Below, the uterine artery pedicle is visible, which is coagulated without cutting.

The surgeon lifts the bladder peritoneum and uses suction irrigation to bluntly separate the bladder from the vagina. The dissection proceeds smoothly due to the absence of adhesions, facilitated by the patient's nulliparous status.
The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from the cervix and vagina, lifting the bladder upwards. The uterus is elevated, and the ligature device is used to coagulate the uterine artery.

The surgeon uses a ligature to coagulate the ascending uterine artery near the uterus on the right side, then moves to the left side to repeat the coagulation process. The strategy involves coagulating both sides before cutting, ensuring control over potential bleeding.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the uterine artery on the left side, revealing the clearly visible lumen of the artery without any bleeding.
The surgeon uses a mac and rot technique to coagulate and cut tissue in the surgical field, focusing on precise manipulation of the uterine structures.

The surgeon begins the colpotomy procedure using a harmonic scalpel, applying coagulation to the lateral margins of the vagina to minimize bleeding. The bladder has already been separated from the vagina.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel and a grasper to hook the cervix upward while a screw pulls the uterus upward. The cervico-vaginal fascia is being carefully separated on the anterior side, with a grasper supporting the entire pedicle from below.

The rectum is visible below and is being supported. The harmonic scalpel is used to cut the vagina, gradually opening the lateral fornix as close to the cervix as possible. The initial few centimeters of the cervix may also be cut during this process, leading to the opening of the vagina.
The surgical field shows the opened vault with the rectum visible below. Multiple fibroids are present in the uterus and fundus, while the cervix area appears stable.
The surgeon appreciates the opening of the vagina following colpotomy, with the vagina visibly opened laterally. A glove is used to pack the vagina to prevent gas leakage.
The surgeon exposes the vaginal mucosa and epithelium, preparing to continue the lateral and anterior colpotomy. The harmonic scalpel is used to cut through half of the uterus.

The surgeon uses a ligature to cut and separate the left half of the vagina, while the right half remains attached. The posterior vaginal wall is visible, and the ligature is preferred for better hemostasis. The lumen of the vagina is clearly observable as the last part of the attached tissue is being addressed.

The surgeon completes the hysterectomy and begins the morcellation process. Large fibroids are being morcellated first, followed by the uterus, using a morcellator device. The size and number of fibroids make it impossible to remove the uterus through the vagina without morcellation.

The surgeon performs morcellation on the uterus, gradually reducing its size. The morcellator is used to fragment the large fibroids and uterine tissue, ensuring the tissue is brought to the morcellator rather than the morcellator being applied directly to the tissue.

The surgeon completes the morcellation process, extracting morselated tissue fragments of the uterus and fibroids. These fragments are collected for pathological examination. The procedure progresses to a bilateral salpingectomy, with the left fallopian tube being carefully separated and removed.
The surgeon completes the bilateral salpingectomy, removing the right fallopian tube through a 15mm modulated port.

The surgeon places a needle with suture through the open vaginal fornix to close the vault. The closure involves intracorporeal suturing using a sergius knot, starting near the uterosacral ligament on the right margin and progressing from the posterior to the anterior vaginal wall.
The surgeon begins the suturing process by taking two wraps followed by a single alternative wrap to form the configuration of a surgeon's knot.

The assistant holds the suture inside the abdomen, facilitating successive bites on the fornix. The uterine artery, which has been coagulated, is visible. Continuous suturing progresses towards the left corner using the same suture tail from the right side.

The surgeon completes the approximation of the corner using a surges knot, providing additional strength to the area. The telescope positioned in the epigastric region offers a view slightly away from the surgical site. The surgeon uses scissors to cut and remove the suture.

The surgeon uses a cobbler's needle to close the morseleter port, pulling the suture from the opposite side to tighten it and include all layers together. The left and right ovaries are inspected, along with the pedicle ovary, confirming everything is intact.
